data.title: Market Voices: Goldman Sachs CEO on AI bubble; Fed's Miran, Goolsbee
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) CEO David Solomon said that stock markets could see a drawdown in the next 24 months in the wake of a massive run-up fueled by investor enthusiasm over AI.  

“Markets run in cycles, and whenever we’ve historically had a significant acceleration in a new technology that creates a lot of capital formation, and therefore lots of interesting new companies around it, you generally see the market run ahead of the potential … there are going to be winners and losers,” Solomon said at the Italian Tech Week conference on Friday, according to CNBC.  

Solomon noted that the dot-com bubble of the late 1990s and early 2000s saw the creation of several of the world's biggest tech companies but also resulted in significant losses for investors. 

“You’re going to see a similar phenomenon here,” he said. “I wouldn’t be surprised if in the next 12 to 24 months, we see a drawdown with respect to equity markets … I think that there will be a lot of capital that’s deployed that will turn out to not deliver returns, and when that happens, people won’t feel good.”

  • Newly appointed Federal Reserve Governor Stephen Miran said he could modify his view on inflation if housing costs climb significantly, but added that rent trends and tighter immigration policies are likely to lower housing inflation.  

“If something were to happen that were to tell me that that channel is invalidated, that there’s some shock that’s going to be pushing rents materially higher, the benign inflation forecast that I have would have to be adjusted as a result,” Miran told Bloomberg TV on Friday.  

Miran is a voting member of the Federal Open Market Committee, or FOMC.

  • Meanwhile Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said Friday that he was "a little wary" about cutting interest rates too quickly due to ongoing concerns about inflation and employment. 

“This uptick of inflation that we’ve been seeing, coupled with the payroll jobs numbers deteriorating, have put the central bank in a bit of a sticky spot where you’re getting deterioration of both sides of the mandate at the same time,” Goolsbee told CNBC on Friday. “I’m a little wary about front-loading too many rate cuts and just counting on the inflation going away.” 

Goolsbee is a voting member of the FOMC.

data.title: AI bubble can still inflate as long as Fed eases - BofA
data.description:
The AI trade can remain "lovely bubbly" with rates going in the right direction, according to BofA.

Every "bubble in history popped by central bank tightening, and no central bank in the world has hiked rates in past 2 months," strategist Michael Hartnett said.

To play the AI bubble, Hartnett said they would barbell AI plays with cheap cyclical assets, going long UK stocks (NYSEARCA:EWU) and commodities (NYSEARCA:DBC) (NASDAQ:PDBC) (NASDAQ:FTGC) (NYSEARCA:COM) (NASDAQ:COMT) (NYSEARCA:USCI), as "AI devours commodities."

data.title: Wall Street edges higher as investors brush aside shutdown worries
data.description:
Wall Street’s broader market averages traded in the green on Friday as market participants shrug off concerns about the U.S. government shutdown for the third day.

Pushing higher was the blue-chip Dow (DJI) as it had climbed up by 0.7%. Also tracking higher on Friday was the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) as it advanced by 0.4%. The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) moved higher by 0.2%. 

On a sector-by-sector stance, eight of the 11 S&P sectors have traded higher, as Utilities leads the way. In reverse, the Communication Services segment has struggled the most and is the weakest sector on the session. 

U.S. Treasury yields ticked higher as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up 2 basis points to 4.10% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed up 2 basis points to 3.56%.

The U.S. government shutdown began at midnight Wednesday after Republicans and Democrats failed to agree on a federal funding deal. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told CNBC on Thursday that the shutdown could weigh on GDP growth.

So far, Wall Street has largely brushed off the impasse. On prediction market Kalshi, the probability of the shutdown lasting more than 10 days rose to 69%.

With federal agencies closed, investors will be left without the September nonfarm payrolls report—a release widely viewed as critical for the Federal Reserve’s assessment of labor market conditions.

In other economic news, S&P Global US Services PMI business activity index came in at 54.2 in September, down from 54.5 in August.

The U.S. ISM Services Index came in at 50.0 in September from 52.0 in August, which was below the 51.6 consensus figure.

Under the magnificent seven umbrella, shares of Apple (AAPL) were downgraded by Jefferies to Underperform from Hold citing excessive expectations on iPhones and foldable phones.

data.title: Wells Fargo sees labor market on a 'knife’s edge' amid untimely shutdown
data.description:
Wells Fargo warned that the federal government shutdown is complicating an already fragile picture for the U.S. labor market. The lack of timely economic data comes as policymakers, investors, and businesses are trying to gauge whether employment conditions can withstand slowing growth.

In an investor note, the investment institution pointed out that the jobs market has shown signs of strain for months. The unemployment rate has crept higher to the upper boundary of what economists consider “full employment.”

While the increase has been gradual, Wells Fargo emphasized that it is occurring alongside a sharp decline in labor force participation and a notable slowdown in hiring momentum. 

“Thus, while the jobs market is not unraveling, it continues to teeter on a knife's edge. Strengthening in end-demand could unleash pent-up demand for workers, but a further moderation or negative shock to growth could tip the scales the other direction, setting off a negative feedback loop between income, spending and hiring,” Wells Fargo said. 

The firm maintained that, barring a prolonged economic disruption, its base case remains that the labor market will endure current headwinds. Still, the shutdown’s delay of key data releases adds another layer of uncertainty for markets and policymakers.

Market Tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

data.title: Bezos admits AI is in 'industrial bubble,' but eventual societal benefits remain 'real'
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) founder and former CEO Jeff Bezos said artificial intelligence is currently in "a kind of industrial bubble" and stock prices are "disconnected from the fundamentals" during a tech conference in Italy on Friday, according to CNBC.

"And investors have a hard time in the middle of this excitement, distinguishing between the good ideas and the bad ideas," he said, according to the report. "And that's also probably happening today ... But that doesn't mean anything that is happening isn't real. AI is real, and it is going to change every industry."

Bezos added that industrial bubbles can be positive, highlighting the biotech bubble of the early 1990s. Despite an eventual market crash after investors poured billions into biotech companies, the investments did lead to the creation of some life-saving medications. 

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman also recently said he thinks the AI industry is currently in a market bubble.

"Are we in a phase where investors as a whole are overexcited about AI? My opinion is yes," he said. "Is AI the most important thing to happen in a very long time? My opinion is also yes."

Bezos made a bold prediction during the on-stage interview at the Italian Tech Week in Turin as well. He said sometime between the next 10 to 20 years, AI data centers will be space-based, orbiting the earth and powered by the sun.

"These giant training clusters, those will be better built in space, because we have solar power there, 24/7," he said, according to Reuters. "There are no clouds and no rain, no weather. We will be able to beat the cost of terrestrial data centers in space in the next couple of decades."

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMAT, RUM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures climbed to fresh records on Friday, positioning Wall Street for solid weekly gains, as investors remained optimistic despite the ongoing U.S. government shutdown and delayed jobs data. 

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Rumble (NASDAQ:RUM) +21% - Shares jumped after the company announced a partnership with AI answer engine Perplexity to integrate AI-powered tools and boost discoverability on its video platform. The deal includes a new bundled subscription combining both companies’ premium plans, while Perplexity’s new product Comet will be promoted across the Rumble ecosystem, including the Rumble Advertising Center. The integration of Perplexity’s search technology into Rumble.com will roll out progressively, though financial terms were undisclosed.
  • USA Rare Earth (NASDAQ:USAR) +21% - Shares soared after CEO Barbara Humpton stated on CNBC that the company is in close communication with President Trump's administration. The news, which followed previous government investments in other critical metals suppliers like MP Materials (MP) and Lithium Americas (LAC), fueled speculation of a potential federal stake in the company. Humpton commented that it will take many players to build out the U.S. rare earth supply chain and that the company is keeping the administration informed of its plans, adding to investor optimism.
  • Red Cat Holdings (RCAT) +18% – Shares jumped after Needham initiated coverage with a Buy rating and $17 PT, citing surging demand for defense-grade drones. Analyst Austin Bohlig said the unmanned aerial systems industry is “entering a multi-year supercycle” and sees Red Cat as well-positioned to benefit. The firm highlighted the U.S. Army’s Short Range Reconnaissance Tranche 2 (SRR2) program as a near-term $200M+ opportunity over the next two years, alongside multi-year tailwinds from expanding defense spending and new domain expansion.

Biggest stock losers

  • Roblox (RBLX) -6% - Shares slid, the sharpest intraday drop since August, after M Science analyst Corey Barrett flagged a sharp deceleration in U.S. bookings growth from recent highs. However, Barrett noted that bookings growth is holding near peak levels in non-core markets (ex-U.S. and Western Europe), while global engagement remains “at or near peak levels.”
  • Bumble (BMBL) -4% – Shares slipped after Goldman Sachs cut its rating to Neutral from Buy, citing uncertainty about the company’s ability to return to growth in the next 12 months. Analyst Eric Sheridan also flagged safety concerns in online dating that could weigh on the platform for up to two years. While constructive on the long-term outlook, Sheridan wants clearer evidence of execution on turnaround plans. His downgrade comes amid a sector-wide review, with Match’s (MTCH) Hinge showing 6% MAU growth and Grindr (GRND) pushing long-term initiatives. He views Bumble’s risk/reward as more balanced relative to peers, despite near-term churn and softer marketing spend.
  • Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) -3% -Shares slipped after the chip equipment maker warned that a new U.S. export rule will further limit its sales to China. The company said that the updated “BIS Affiliates Rule” could cut its fourth-quarter revenue by approximately $110M and its FY2026 revenue by around $600M. This new restriction adds to the pressure on the company, which has already trailed its peers this year due to its significant exposure to the Chinese market.

data.title: Capesize shipping rates sink with China’s ban on BHP iron ore cargoes
data.description:
Dry bulk shipping rates have plunged ~25% this week due to a dispute over BHP's (NYSE:BHP) iron ore shipments to China and a seasonal lull in demand, Bloomberg reported Friday.

Spot rates for Capesize bulk carriers fell 6% on Thursday to the lowest since late August, extending declines seen earlier this week after Bloomberg reported that China's state-run iron ore buyer had ordered a temporary halt to new purchases of dollar-denominated seaborne cargoes from BHP (NYSE:BHP).

Capesize markets are sensitive to movements in the iron ore market, as iron ore prices jumped nearly 15% last month from June lows as a result of China’s push to curb overcapacity.

Both sides have an interest in ending the dispute quickly; shipbroker Fearnleys said in a report this week that China buys as much as 250 million tons/year of iron ore from BHP (BHP), compared to a total of 1.2 billion tons purchased last year.

Australia has the upper hand given its low-cost supply and BHP's (BHP) sizeable share of the market, Capital Economics analyst Marcel Thieliant said, adding that other companies would not be able to fill the gap, and any upward pressure on prices would be bad news for China given how many mills are already unprofitable.

data.title: Industrials Q3 roundup: CHRW, CMI tops chart, CARR, UPS worst performer
data.description:
The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLI), which tracks S&P 500 Industrial Index (SP500-20) extended its gains in the third quarter.

The industrial index gained 4.5%, after having gained 12.5% in Q2, while the broader index S&P 500 ETF (SPY) gained 7.8% in Q3.

All S&P 500 Industrial industries ended in green. Capital Goods (SP500-2010), which holds highest weightage to the index, ended quarter with 6.5% gain, Transportation (SP500-2030) climbed 4.0% and Commercial & Professional Services (SP500-2020) lost 4.5% during Q3 period. 

Top 5 movers of Q3:

Gainers:

C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) +34.7%. 

Cummins (CMI) +25.7%

EMCOR Group (EME) +22.3%.

General Electric (GE) +22.2%.

Northrop Grumman (NOC) +22.1%.

Losers:

Carrier Global (CARR) -21.2%.

United Parcel Service (UPS) -21.1%.

Verisk Analytics (VRSK) -19.6%.

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) -16.4%.

Deere & Company (DE) -12.6%.

What Quantitative Measures say?

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating has given XLI a BUY rating, with a score of 3.64 out of 5. The rating system has given the industrial sector tracking fund an A+ in liquidity and expenses, A in dividends, B+ in momentum and a D in risk.

Fund Flows

The Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF recorded a Q3 inflow of $927.9M compared to inflow of $418.92M in the second quarter.

data.title: Bumble downgraded at Goldman as growth, safety issues weigh on outlook
data.description:
Goldman Sachs is removing its Buy rating on Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) as uncertainty remains about the company’s ability to return to growth over the next 12 months and as safety concerns surrounding on-line dating could weigh on the platform for the next 18 to 24 months.

“While we remain constructive on the long-term opportunity for Bumble, we wait for further proof points around execution on these operational turnaround efforts,” analyst Eric Sheridan writes in his research note to clients.

Sheridan’s downgrade on Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) is part of a broader look at the on-line data space with Match (MTCH), Grindr (GRND), and Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) all heading into third quarter results. While the results are important, Sheridan also finds investors are equally concerned about the ability of the three to strike a balance between user growth, engagement, and monetization, the impact that AI will have on user engagement, and the scope for investments in long-term secular growth themes while also producing solid/stable margins.

Sheridan’s Neutral rating on Bumble (BMBL) reflects not only the short-term challenges the company faces from increased churn and reduced marketing spending, but the favorable risk/reward relative to Match (MTCH) and Grindr (GRND) with Match’s Hinge achieving 6% growth in monthly average users (MAU) and Grindr’s (GRND) initiatives to achieve long-term growth targets.

Goldman’s downgrade is weighing on Bumble (BMBL) shares with the stock down 3% to a 4-month low.

data.title: Walmart-backed OnePay will offer crypto later this year – report
data.description:
The Walmart-backed fintech firm OnePay will start offering cryptocurrency trading and custody on its mobile app, according to CNBC.

OnePay, which is majority-owned by Walmart (NYSE:WMT), is teaming up with Zerohash to offer access to Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) later this year. Customers could be able to convert cryptocurrencies into cash and use those funds to make purchases or pay off card balances, a CNBC report said.

OnePay was founded by Walmart (NYSE:WMT) and Ribbit Capital in 2021 and has added new products such as WeChat – a Chinese social media and instant messaging payment app developed by Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY).

OnePay also offers banking services such as high-yield savings accounts, credit cards and debit cards, and ‘buy now, pay later’ services, as well as integrated on-line and in-person checkout processes at Walmart’s U.S. locations.

Last month, Morgan Stanley (MS) also teamed up with Zerohash to offer cryptocurrency to its E*Trade customers starting in 2026.

Zerohash raised $104M in funding last month from firms including Morgan Stanley (MS) and Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

Under President Donald Trump’s administration, the Federal Reserve, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation withdrew restrictive crypto guidance from 2023, signaling a major policy shift toward integrating digital assets into the mainstream banking system.
